trigger.reason: OpenAI announced GPT-5.6, a new model release, which meets all trigger criteria despite government-imposed restrictions on rollout; this is a major capability milestone likely to trigger sympathy-fade selling across AI-adjacent software sectors.

primary_event.headline: OpenAI limits GPT-5.6 rollout after government request, says restrictions shouldn't be the norm
primary_event.summary: OpenAI has released GPT-5.6, a new model, but is limiting its initial rollout to a select group of partners at the White House's request due to safety concerns. The company has publicly objected to the restriction, arguing that such government access processes shouldn't become the norm.

secondary_events.headline: OpenAI's Jalapeño chip is Big Tech's spiciest move away from Nvidia

discovery.trigger_acknowledgment: Analyzed OpenAI GPT-5.6 model release (rollout limited at White House request) as the AI sympathy-fade trigger affecting customer-service, edtech, SaaS, cybersecurity, legal tech, and adjacent verticals.
discovery.run_summary: The trigger drove a classic AI-sympathy-fade pattern: vertical SaaS and healthcare workflow names rallied (likely a relief bounce or simply rotation) while semi-cap names sold off on AI-capex-peak fears. The sell-side names (AEIS, PLAB, ONTO) are mechanically backwards reactions — a model release with constrained rollout doesn't reduce AI training/inference compute demand, and these names supply the picks-and-shovels for that demand.

discoveries.name: Advanced Energy Industries, Inc
discoveries.filings_evidence: 10-K explicitly states: 'infrastructure investments in artificial intelligence (AI) have increased substantially, which is driving significant demand increases in the Data Center Computing market. We accelerated investments to increase capacity and make upgrades to support higher demand and new product requirements in the market.' AEIS is a direct AI capex beneficiary, not a victim of a model release.
discoveries.thesis: AEIS sells precision power conversion for semiconductor fabs and data center computing. An OpenAI model release — especially one with restricted rollout — does nothing to reduce demand for the power-delivery infrastructure underneath AI training/inference. The selloff is mechanically backwards: more AI capability deployment = more data center power = more AEIS content.
discoveries.what_confirms: Continued data center bookings strength in next 10-Q; hyperscaler capex guides unchanged or raised; semi-cap peers (KLA, LRCX, AMAT) recover without similar magnitude drawdowns.
discoveries.what_kills: An actual hyperscaler capex cut announcement, a specific AEIS guide-down on data center segment, or a customer concentration disclosure showing dependence on a single OpenAI-adjacent buyer.
discoveries.what_to_learn: Semi-cap and power-infra names that supply AI buildout often sell off on AI-lab news — this is a recurring backwards trade. Picks-and-shovels exposure is the opposite of the panic narrative.
discoveries.catalyst: OpenAI limits GPT-5.6 rollout after government request, says restrictions shouldn't be the norm
discoveries.catalyst_evidence: AEIS is in the semi-cap/data-center power complex that retail/algos lump into 'AI plays' — a constrained model release feeds an AI-capex-peak narrative even though AEIS supplies the physical buildout.
research_pointers: Verify recent data center segment growth rate and backlog from latest 10-Q
Check hyperscaler capex guides from MSFT/META/GOOGL/AMZN earnings post-trigger
Compare AEIS five-day move to KLAC/LRCX/AMAT for sympathy-fade dispersion

discoveries.name: Photronics, Inc.
discoveries.filings_evidence: Risk Factors describe Photronics as photomask supplier dependent on 'design activity' in semiconductors — every new chip design, including AI accelerators, requires photomasks. No language identifies AI model releases as a threat; the actual risks cited are cyclicality and customer concentration, not AI substitution.
discoveries.thesis: Photronics makes photomasks — the templates used to manufacture every chip, including every AI accelerator and HBM die. An OpenAI model release with restricted rollout has zero substitutive relationship to mask demand; if anything, more advanced AI models require more advanced nodes, which require more complex (and more expensive) masks. Pure semi-sympathy sell.
discoveries.what_confirms: Foundry capex commentary from TSMC/Samsung shows continued advanced-node investment; PLAB's IC mask revenue (especially high-end) stays strong in next print.
discoveries.what_kills: A specific customer order cancellation, a step-down in advanced-node mask pricing, or evidence that AI accelerator designs are consolidating around fewer mask sets.
discoveries.what_to_learn: Photomask makers are leveraged to chip DESIGN cycles, not chip USAGE cycles. AI model releases stimulate design activity (custom silicon, ASICs) which is upstream tailwind for PLAB.
discoveries.catalyst: OpenAI limits GPT-5.6 rollout after government request, says restrictions shouldn't be the norm
discoveries.catalyst_evidence: PLAB is in the broader semi complex that gets sold on any AI-capex-peak narrative despite being a design-cycle beneficiary.
research_pointers: Check PLAB IC high-end mask revenue mix in latest 10-Q
Cross-reference with TSMC N2/N3 ramp commentary
Look for AI accelerator customer wins disclosed in recent investor updates

discoveries.name: Onto Innovation Inc.
discoveries.filings_evidence: 10-K discusses dependence on 'semiconductor device manufacturing' and the need for inspection/metrology/lithography systems at advanced nodes. The Risk Factors describe cyclical industry exposure but contain no language identifying AI model releases as a substitutive threat; the company's process-control tools are required equipment for advanced-node AI chip production.
discoveries.thesis: Onto sells process control (inspection, metrology, lithography) for advanced semiconductor manufacturing including HBM and advanced packaging — both of which are critical to AI accelerator production. A model release shipped with constrained access has no bearing on metrology tool demand. AI chip complexity is increasing process control intensity per wafer, which is structurally bullish for Onto.
discoveries.what_confirms: HBM-related metrology revenue continues to grow; advanced packaging tool orders from major foundries stay robust in next print.
discoveries.what_kills: A foundry-specific capex cut affecting advanced packaging, customer concentration disclosure revealing high single-customer AI exposure that gets reduced, or guide-down tied to specific AI accelerator program.
discoveries.what_to_learn: Process control is a per-wafer-complexity play, not a per-wafer-volume play. AI chips (HBM stacks, chiplets, advanced packaging) need more inspection steps, making Onto a structural AI capex winner.
discoveries.catalyst: OpenAI limits GPT-5.6 rollout after government request, says restrictions shouldn't be the norm
discoveries.catalyst_evidence: ONTO is in the semi-cap process-control complex that trades in sympathy with AI sentiment despite being upstream of chip-complexity (not chip-quantity) demand.
research_pointers: Check ONTO's HBM and advanced packaging segment commentary in latest 10-Q
Compare drawdown vs KLAC (closest pure process-control peer)
Review Rigaku acquisition rationale for AI metrology positioning

_trigger.headline: OpenAI limits GPT-5.6 rollout after government request, says restrictions shouldn't be the norm
_trigger.summary: OpenAI has released GPT-5.6, a new model, but is limiting its initial rollout to a select group of partners at the White House's request due to safety concerns. The company has publicly objected to the restriction, arguing that such government access processes shouldn't become the norm.
